1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/624a792/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F38%2Fec22f4be413aa8d1b1995c9dc7e9%2F1-loc-butterfly-evan-leroy-johnson-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/93e79d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F38%2Fec22f4be413aa8d1b1995c9dc7e9%2F1-loc-butterfly-evan-leroy-johnson-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Madama Butterfly Dress Rehearsal" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/93e79d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F38%2Fec22f4be413aa8d1b1995c9dc7e9%2F1-loc-butterfly-evan-leroy-johnson-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/46f7f20/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F38%2Fec22f4be413aa8d1b1995c9dc7e9%2F1-loc-butterfly-evan-leroy-johnson-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/93e79d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F38%2Fec22f4be413aa8d1b1995c9dc7e9%2F1-loc-butterfly-evan-leroy-johnson-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>B.F. Pinkerton (Evan LeRoy Johnson) dons a virtual reality headset and enters a game that sets the whole story in motion with him in the role of his avatar — a Navy lieutenant at the beginning of the 20th century arriving in Nagasaki aboard his warship, the Abraham Lincoln.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Todd Rosenberg Photography</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The apartment splits in half and slides to the left and right of the stage, and the virtual reality world — which both draws on Japanese tradition (including an enlarged version of a Hokusai depiction of Mt. Fuji as an interior backdrop) and defies it — slides into place in the middle. But with the side sections taking up at least a third of the stage, the action is oddly compressed and sometimes crowded in this central area — a major downside to the design by Kimie Nishikawa and the dots collective.</p><p>Garish, sometimes flashing lights and costumes (designer Maiko Matsushima) with their oddly cartoonish looks and dashes of lime green inspired by contemporary Japanese pop culture are meant to play up the artificiality of the world of Puccini and his collaborators. But by doing so, this approach doesn’t allow viewers to invest in the emotions of the traditional story and turns the production into a kind of intellectual exercise in repatriation.</p><p>Conductor Domingo Hindoyan, music director designate of the Los Angeles Opera, has the tough task of trying to achieve cohesion among the production’s disparate, competing elements, and for the most part, he succeeds. But throughout this production, there is a constant uncomfortable tension between Puccini’s music, which wants to go in one direction, and the staging, which clearly wants to go in another.</p><p>Nowhere is this break more evident than at the end. Instead of committing suicide in her home after her child has been taken from her, Butterfly (Cio-Cio San) flees the set. She runs to the front of the stage to complete the act, making her death a bid to escape the repressive confines of the entire opera — perhaps the most inspired dramatic moment in this production. But this move inevitably ruins the final emotional effect of Puccini’s opera, and perhaps that is the point.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-840000" name="image-840000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/53ee991/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2Fea%2Fab95347d469d8e9d95ba3f5ab4d1%2F28-loc-butterfly-karah-son-nozomi-kato-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b9784d1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2Fea%2Fab95347d469d8e9d95ba3f5ab4d1%2F28-loc-butterfly-karah-son-nozomi-kato-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8aceabf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2Fea%2Fab95347d469d8e9d95ba3f5ab4d1%2F28-loc-butterfly-karah-son-nozomi-kato-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Nozomi Kato portrays Suzuki, the loyal servant of Cio-Cio-San (Karah Son)." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8aceabf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2Fea%2Fab95347d469d8e9d95ba3f5ab4d1%2F28-loc-butterfly-karah-son-nozomi-kato-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3872205/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2Fea%2Fab95347d469d8e9d95ba3f5ab4d1%2F28-loc-butterfly-karah-son-nozomi-kato-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8aceabf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2Fea%2Fab95347d469d8e9d95ba3f5ab4d1%2F28-loc-butterfly-karah-son-nozomi-kato-c-todd-rosenberg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Nozomi Kato portrays Suzuki, the loyal servant of Cio-Cio-San (Karah Son).</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Todd Rosenberg Photography</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But if so, it is easy to wonder if it wouldn’t have made more sense to create an entirely new opera that would take “Madama Butterfly” as a starting point for the cultural, ethnic and historical explorations Ozawa desires.</p><p>Almost lost or at least overshadowed amid the dramatic reworking of this opera are the two fine, well-matched singers in the principal roles, starting with Korean soprano Karah Son, who is making her Lyric debut as Butterfly.</p><p>She has sung this role many times and her comfort with it is evident as she persuasively conveys both the youthfulness and maturity of Butterfly. Her penetrating, forceful voice rings out easily when she first enters from the far rear of the stage in the bridal procession.</p><p>Making his Lyric debut as well, the tall, strapping American tenor, Evan LeRoy Johnson, certainly looks the part of Navy Lt. Pinkerton. He has a natural, compelling feel for Puccini’s musical lines, soaring securely to the role’s top notes.</p><p>The rest of the cast is solid, including Japanese mezzo-soprano Nozomi Kato, as Suzuki, Butterfly’s loyal servant, and baritone Zachary Nelson as Sharpless, the American consul who arranges the marriage and has prescient misgivings about all that is to follow.</p>
entries.author: Kyle MacMillan

entries.title: Coroner: 4-year-old dies in Mundelein house fire
entries.summary: <p>A 4-year-old child is dead following a house fire in Mundelein overnight, according the Lake County coroner’s office.</p><p>An autopsy is scheduled for later today.</p><p>Fire officials said multiple people attempted to make entry into the home on the 200 block of Dalton Avenue, but were driven back because of heavy fire and smoke.</p><p>The stairwell to the basement had also burned away, hampering efforts to rescue the child, fire officials said.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260323/news/report-4-year-old-dies-in-mundelein-house-fire/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.<br></p>

entries.title: Can replacing Illinois' toxic lead pipes lead to a workforce boom?
entries.summary: <p><i>This coverage is made possible through a partnership between WBEZ and</i><a class="Link" href="http://grist.org/" target="_blank" ><i> Grist</i></a><i>, a nonprofit environmental media organization.</i></p><p>Illinois is in the midst of a public health crisis. Nearly 1.5 million service lines — the pipes that carry drinking water to homes and businesses — contain or are suspected to contain lead, a neurotoxin linked to cognitive, reproductive, and cardiovascular problems.</p><p>Now, public health and workforce advocates want to turn the state’s long-overdue pipe replacement backlog into a statewide economic engine, creating up to 90,000 jobs over a decade.</p><p>A recent <a class="Link" href="https://metroplanning.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/Leveraging-LSLR-for-Workforce-Inclusion-Growth-Report-Action-Steps.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>report</u></a> proposes a plan to replace the state's staggering inventory of toxic lead pipes and create tens of thousands of jobs. To do so, the analysis calls on state and local officials to fast-track pipe replacements for communities that have suffered from the most lead exposure and to use the projects to build a more diverse local workforce. It also urges the Illinois General Assembly to help plug a multi-billion-dollar budget gap for lead pipe replacements.</p><p>“The longer we put off taking care of our water infrastructure, the more expensive it's going to get, the more that we're going to be looking at water rates increasing to deal with that, and the more people are going to be in the position where they're not going to have access to safe and clean drinking water,” said Justin Williams, a senior manager at the Metropolitan Planning Council, one of the policy think tanks that helped develop the plan. “And that's not a situation we should be in as a state or region.”</p><p>Several other regional and national nonprofits, including Current, a water solutions hub; Elevate, an organization working on water and energy affordability issues; and HIRE360, a workforce development group, also worked on the analysis.</p><p>Illinois has the most lead pipes in the country. The state estimates it has 667,000 known lead service lines and another 820,000 suspected lines. Chicago alone accounts for nearly 30 percent of those pipes.</p><p>Replacing these service lines is expensive. In a <a class="Link" href="https://epa.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/epa/topics/drinking-water/public-water-users/documents/lead-service-line-replacement-advisory-board/presentation-2.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>2022 report</u></a>, the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency found that a single service line replacement can cost anywhere from $4,000 to $13,000 across the state. In Chicago, the price tag is even higher: City officials estimated that replacements cost more than $30,000 per line on average.</p><p>State officials have estimated that replacing all the known or suspected lead pipes across Illinois could cost between $6 and $10 billion. The Biden-era Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, also known as the bipartisan infrastructure law, set aside $15 billion over five years to help states replace lead pipes. Illinois is estimated to receive about <a class="Link" href="https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6075efd94bf3ee2bc0024798/6840a7d1fe1cff00fb6907c0_IL%20BIL%20Water%20Infrastructure%20Fact%20Sheet%20V2.1.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>$1 billion</u></a>, but given the state’s unique needs, that number “is probably on the low side,” Williams said.</p><p>The report makes the case that state lawmakers must approve dedicated, sustained, and predictable funding to close the multi-billion-dollar shortfall. Without long-term guarantees, replacements will likely remain inefficient and delayed.</p><p>“It's a bit of a chicken and egg: Unless you know how much money is going to be allocated to this — how many opportunities are coming down the pipe — they're not going to add additional people to apprenticeship programs,” said Jay Rowell, executive director at HIRE360.</p><p>Using workforce projections from the American Water Works Association and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, the report’s authors calculated that already allocated federal funds could generate approximately 2,000 direct jobs and 9,000 indirect jobs. If legislatures closed the multi-billion-dollar funding gap, those figures could jump substantially to 35,000 direct jobs and 55,000 indirect jobs — a total of 90,000 jobs over a decade.</p><p>“We're calling attention not only to the problem, but also to some of the opportunities to get more candidates engaged in apprenticeships,” said Rowell. “This is a really big problem that needs very thoughtful, state-led solutions.”</p><p>A major pillar of the report is diversifying the building trades. An analysis of Chicago’s workforce found that only 3.8 percent of registered apprentices are women and just 10 percent are Black. To bridge this gap, the report advocates for requiring utilities and municipalities to include diversity and equity requirements in project contracts.</p><p>The report’s authors argue that Illinois has the rare opportunity to tackle two challenges at once: address its toxic legacy while laying the groundwork for a more inclusive economy. The financial and political hurdles remain high, but advocates say the cost of inaction is higher.</p><p>“We are the envy of the world in terms of our access to fresh drinking water,” said Williams. “We need to be really thoughtful stewards of that, and that means investing in that the same way we invest in other infrastructure.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/environment/2025/08/28/find-chicago-lead-pipes-home-apartment" >Lead pipe problem the worst in Chicago’s majority Black and Latino neighborhoods </a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/environment/2025/08/28/map-chicago-lead-service-line-water-equity-what-we-learned" >How we mapped Chicago’s lead service lines and what we learned</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/environment/2025/06/26/chicago-toxic-lead-service-line-pipes-replacement-delayed-biden-trump" >Many Chicagoans likely to wait decades for dangerous lead pipes to be replaced</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>

entries.title: Chicago Lebanese-Americans mourn war victims

class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Karl El Sokhn a Lebanese Albany Park resident stands in his neighborhood, Friday, March 20, 2026. Sokhn has family and friends currently living in Lebanon who are enduring the Israeli bombardment.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-h/" target="_blank" >Lebanese Americans mourn dead, try to help loved ones as 1M displaced by Israeli strikes</a></h3><h5></h5><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-k/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p><b>Impacts of war</b>: Thousands of Lebanese Americans — about 15,000 in Illinois, the state with the sixth-largest Arab population — are watching their home country come under fire as the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran continues to ignite the region.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Israeli strikes have killed at least 1,500 people in Iran and more than 1,000 in Lebanon after the Lebanese militant group Hezbollah fired on Israel, while Iranian strikes have killed at least 15 in Israel, according to officials in those countries. Israeli fire has displaced more than a million people in Lebanon, a year since the last conflict uprooted a million Lebanese residents.</p><p><b>Chicagoans watch on</b>: Three of Rodolph Saliba’s friends in Lebanon have been killed by rockets in recent weeks, the Oak Lawn resident said. More are sleeping in tents or going hungry. "There are a lot of innocent people who don’t deserve this," Saliba said. "I just hope this war stops." Muhammad Sankari, a Southwest Side resident, has spent weeks checking on family and friends. "It’s difficult to be so far removed when your loved ones are experiencing these horrors," Sankari said. "Even if the bombs stop tomorrow, there’s a huge economic and humanitarian impact."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-h/" target="_blank" ><b>READ href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-o/" target="_blank" >TSA workers push back, demand pay as Trump plans to deploy ICE agents in airports</a></h3><h5></h5><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-b/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p><b>ICE at airports</b>: After President Donald Trump threatened to deploy U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents to airports amid a Department of Homeland Security funding fight in Congress, Transportation Security Administration union workers in the Midwest are expressing concern about agents’ lack of training and their own lack of pay.</p><p><b>Funding fight</b>: Trump made it clear Sunday he would use immigration officers for airport security starting Monday unless Democrats agreed on a bill to fund the DHS. Democrats are demanding major changes in immigration agents' conduct. Hundreds of thousands of homeland security workers, including those from the TSA, have worked without pay since Congress failed to renew DHS funding last month.</p><p><b>The concerns</b>: Everett Kelley, president of the American Federation of Government Employees, which represents TSA agents and other federal workers in Illinois, said immigration agents’ deployment raises security concerns for passengers. Darrell English, president of AFGE Local 777, which represents Chicago TSA workers, cited the agents’ lack of training in aviation security and said they could slow the screening process for travelers.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MUSIC 🎶</h4><div class="Enhancement" </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-m/" target="_blank" >Jurors side with Chance the Rapper in $3.8M legal battle with ex-manager</a></h3><h5></h5><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-c/" target="_blank" >Selena Fragassi</a></h5><p><b>Jury rules</b>: The five-year-plus legal battle between Chance The Rapper, real name Chancelor Bennett, and his former manager, Pat Corcoran, ended Friday. A Cook County jury found Corcoran had failed to prove he was owed the $3.8 million in unpaid commissions and royalties he sought in his lawsuit against his former friend and business partner.</p><p><b>$35 in damages</b>: Jurors also ruled in Bennett's countersuit, which was combined with Corcoran’s lawsuit for purposes of the trial. Bennett had originally sought $1 million in damages. The jury decided Corcoran must pay him $35, and recommended that Corcoran turn over the internet domain name ChanceRaps.com, which Corcoran used to sell Chance the Rapper merchandise.</p><p><b>Fraught relationship</b>: Over their eight-year working relationship, Bennett and Corcoran had been the industry model for a successful, independent artist-manager partnership without record label interference. But as both parties rested their cases, a different narrative emerged. Corcoran’s legal team described a hardworking manager who deserved to be paid for contributing to the ascent of the Grammy-winning rapper. Bennett’s attorneys tried to portray him as an opportunistic ex-employee who was fired for a reason.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-m/" target="_blank" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-w/" target="_blank" >Steppenwolf Theatre gets Sondheim Foundation grant to restart program for new plays</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-yd/" target="_blank" >Mike Davis</a></h5><p><b>New plays boost</b>: Steppenwolf Theatre will reboot a program that supports new plays after receiving a grant from the Stephen Sondheim Foundation. Neither the theater company nor the foundation would disclose the monetary amount of the grant.</p><p><b>Rebuild plans</b>: Steppenwolf executive director Brooke Flanagan says the grant will go toward rebuilding its Scout program, which was shuttered during the COVID-19 pandemic. In its 50-year history, Steppenwolf has developed and launched more than 130 plays. The Scout program partners playwrights with Steppenwolf’s new play development team. It will return for the theater’s 2026-2027 season.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: "I think we are really thrilled to be able to have the program back up and running, and we know that we’re looking forward to the collaboration and what it means to the industry and to the artists that are involved," Flanagan said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-w/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX ⚾️🏒🏀</h4><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-19d14bd0-26bc-11f1-9bf7-a3705d28a66e"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-yh/" target="_blank" ><b>Odd man out, another in</b></a>: The White Sox signed catcher Reese McGuire to a one-year, $1.2 million contract. Calbert Wright, who’s been working at Ford’s Chicago Heights plant since 1963</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-ji/" target="_blank" >Neil Steinberg</a></h5><p>He didn't think he'd stick around. When Calbert Wright began work at the Ford Motor Company’s Chicago Stamping Plant in Chicago Heights in 1963, the factory was a noisy, smelly, smoky hellscape with a leaky roof. Plus Black workers, such as himself, were given the hardest duties. But stay he did.</p><p>When Wright, at the age of 23, began work at Ford, John F. Kennedy was president. Henry Ford II, grandson of the man who founded the automobile manufacturer in 1903, still ran the business. That means Wright, who walks the floor today, has worked for Ford for more than half the 123 years since the company sold its first car, a two-cylinder, two-passenger Model A, in red, for $850 to Ernest Pfennig, a dentist on Clybourn Avenue.</p><p>Why did he stay?</p><p>“There weren’t jobs paying like this,” he said, laughing: $1.40 an hour. “Big money.” He had a wife, Thelma — now married 65 years — and an infant son to consider. And things were changing. "[Martin Luther] King, plus the union, made everybody be classified,” Wright said. Conditions improved. He moved up from stacking steel. “That’s why I stayed so long.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wtitkiy-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>READ entries.title: ICE agents spotted at O’Hare Airport as partial government shutdown continues to impact travelers
entries.summary: <p>U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers were present at O'Hare International Airport Monday, after the Trump administration said agents would be deployed to airports nationwide to help amid the ongoing partial government shutdown.</p><p>At least three agents in ICE gear were seen at Terminal 3 around 10 a.m. Monday morning.</p><p>A day after saying he would use immigration officers for <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/government-shutdown-tsa-workers-920577c153ffd1877f59dfb8f8efed39" target="_blank" ><u>airport security</u></a> starting Monday unless Democrats agreed on a bill to fund the <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/us-department-of-homeland-security" target="_blank" ><u>Department of Homeland Security</u></a>, Trump made clear Sunday that he was going ahead with the plan to assist the Transportation Security Administration.</p><p>O’Hare was among 13 airports included in a list of where ICE agents would be present to assist, <a class="Link" href="https://www.cnn.com/us/live-news/tsa-wait-times-ice-airports-03-23-26?post-id=cmn37qf65000q3b6rfo32wpep" target="_blank" >according to CNN</a>. Department of Homeland Security acting assistant secretary Lauren Bis said in a statement, “For operational security reasons, we are not going to confirm the locations of our officers," but flagged nine major airports, including Baltimore/Washington International Airport in Maryland and John F. Kennedy Airport International in New York as having high TSA worker callout rates on Sunday. O'Hare and Midway Airports were not on that list.</p><p>Luke Hoover and Mary Dickey, of North Center, were on their way to New Zealand for their honeymoon and got to O'Hare three hours early for their flight after bracing for possible delays stemming from the partial government shutdown. </p><p>"It gives us pause, [we're] not a big fan of it," Hoover said of ICE agents at O'Hare. "We figured with that plus TSA just being a bit of a mess lately, we'd get here early and ready to go." </p><p>"Just [the] TSA process alone, having people who aren’t trained in the process trying to, you know, expedite the process, just doesn’t make sense to me," Dickey added. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-aa0000" name="image-aa0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/505c614/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa1%2F2f%2Fad1ed7bb4af99baa29426f318261%2Fairportice-260324-17.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c14838a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa1%2F2f%2Fad1ed7bb4af99baa29426f318261%2Fairportice-260324-17.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents stand guard at the exit point of the arrival area in terminal one at O’hare International Airpot, Monday, March 23, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Abdul Qahar Karimi saw two armed ICE agents as he exited his flight to Chicago from Omaha, Nebraska. Karimi, a 27-year-old truck driver in town for work, had friends who were green card holders detained in Omaha.</p><p>"[The ICE agents] didn’t ask me, or didn’t tell me anything or search me," Karimi said.</p><p>Though the agents being there didn’t make Karimi feel any less safe, he still prefers they be away from airports across the country.</p><p>"They shouldn’t be here because the airport itself has its own security, their own police," Karimi, 27, said. "They're doing violent things to U.S. citizens and citizens from other countries, so I don’t think it's proper [for them] to be here." </p><p>White House border czar Tom Homan said during Sunday news show interviews that ICE's increased role at airports was subject to discussions with the leadership of the agency and the TSA “to find out where we can fit in.” Immigration officers, as an example, could cover exits currently monitored by TSA agents, freeing them to work screening lines, or have ICE agents check identification before people enter screenings areas.</p><p>During an appearance on Chris Cuomo’s SiriusXM show Monday, Homan expanded on how ICE agents will work with airport security and predicted that agents will be met with protesters.</p><p>"We're going to first send out to the biggest airports with the biggest wait lines. Homan told the program’s executive producer, Alexandra “Dusty” Cohen.</p><p>When asked if that meant he was already preparing for protests, Homan responded, "I'm sure there will be. You know in the bigger cities where there's been issues in the past, I expect there's going to be protests outside the airport.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e50000" name="image-e50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/22308cf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64bb613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="AIRPORTICE_260324-03.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64bb613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9b605fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64bb613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Travelers wait in line at terminal one at O’hare International Airpot, Monday, March 23, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>No ICE agents were spotted at Midway, but some travelers still felt impacted.</p><p>Anayeli Castro, who was waiting for her husband by the security check point with her two young kids, had heard online that they should arrive at least four hours early. She tried checking the TSA app to see how long the security line was but the app was not updating the wait time.</p><p>“I'm here three hours before, luckily the lines are not long right now,” Castro said. “We normally arrive two hours early but I when heard you have to be here 4 to 5 hours early, I was freaking out.”</p><p>She also saw on social media that ICE agents could be deployed to Chicago airports.</p><p>“They're ICE agents. It's different from TSA, so it does kind of freak you out a little bit," Castro said. “I am an immigrant, so it's scary but it seems pretty calm right now.”</p><p>Lori Houzer found a place to sit at Midway Airport while she waited to check her bags for her flight to Canada. She said she followed recommended instructions to arrive three to four hours beforehand but despite the early arrival there are no staff to check in passengers.</p><p>“I would have arrived max two hours early if I'm checking luggage, which is more than enough time, sometimes an hour and a half,” she said. “Now it's at least two hours more, and that's if things go well,” she said.</p><p>Houzer said she kept up with the news and knew TSA agents were not getting paid due to the government shutdown.</p><p>“People should get paid because not only is it incorrect to ask people who already aren't earning very much money to get no money, but also what is their motivation for doing their job really well?” she said. “It's a security job. I would like people to take it seriously.”</p><p>She isn’t worried about ICE agents being at airports, but she does think it’s a bad use of funds.</p><p>“They're gonna pay ICE but they're not gonna pay regular people?” she questioned. “I'm also Canadian. I don't really understand it very well.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/22/union-tsa-workers-push-back-demand-pay-after-trump-admin-deploys-ice-agents-in-airports-monday" target="_blank" >TSA union officials raised concerns</a> over passenger security with ICE agents present.</p><p>“Our members at TSA have been showing up every day, without a paycheck, because they believe in the mission of keeping the flying public safe,” Everett Kelley, the national president of the American Federation of Government Employees, said in a statement Sunday. “They deserve to be paid, not replaced by untrained, armed agents who have shown how dangerous they can be.”</p><p><i>This is a developing story. Check back for updates.</i></p><p><i>Contributing Violet Miller</i></p>
entries.author: Araceli Gómez-Aldana | WBEZ

entries.title: Trump decries Loyola student's killing while pushing deportations; suspect will likely miss court
entries.summary: <p>As President Donald Trump used the “devastating” killing of Loyola University Chicago student Sheridan Gorman to justify his push for mass deportations on Monday, the alleged gunman remained hospitalized and was expected to miss his initial court hearing later in the day.</p><p>Jose G. Medina, a Venezuelan immigrant, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >charged with first-degree murder</a>, attempted murder, aggravated discharge of a firearm, and illegal gun possession in connection to the March 19 attack near Tobey Prinz Beach, Cook County court records show.</p><p>Trump told reporters Monday that he had been briefed on Gorman’s killing, arguing that the fatal shooting showed there was a need for ramped up immigration enforcement efforts.</p><p>“This person came in through the open door policy of [former President] Joe Biden and we have others,” Trump said. “ We’re taking them out by the tens of thousands. We’re doing a great job, but it’s a shame.”</p><p>Medina, 25, was wanted on a warrant in a local shoplifting case at the time of his latest arrest.</p><p>Gorman’s family said they were “gravely disappointed by the policies and failures that allowed this individual to remain in a position to commit this crime."</p><p>“When systems fail — whether through release decisions, lack of coordination, or unwillingness to act — the consequences are not abstract. They are real,” the family said in a statement.</p><p>“And in our case, they are permanent. This case must be prosecuted to the fullest extent of both state and federal law. There can be no gaps, no shortcuts, and no second chances that put others at risk. Accountability must be complete.”</p><p>Gorman, 18, and her friends took an early morning walk Thursday to the beach just north of Loyola’s campus to take pictures of the skyline, according to her family.</p><p>When they arrived, a masked gunman “emerged from the shadows” and fired a single shot as they fled, according to police records. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/19/woman-18-shot-to-death-in-predawn-rogers-park-attack" target="_blank" >Gorman was struck in the back</a> as she attempted to flee. Police initially reported she’d been struck in the head. </p><p>One of Gorman’s friends who witnessed the shooting <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/20/chicago-loyola-sheridan-gorman-rogers-park-crime-shooting-murder" target="_blank" >told the Chicago Sun-Times</a> that “it didn’t seem like it was intentionally targeted toward any of us.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f80000" name="image-f80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" to death, Thursday, March 19, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5068012/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6818x3826+0+606/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2F30%2F872fd020461881bad76323c2496f%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/520538c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6818x3826+0+606/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2F30%2F872fd020461881bad76323c2496f%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5068012/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6818x3826+0+606/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2F30%2F872fd020461881bad76323c2496f%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Police Department officers investigate on the pier at Tobey Prinz Beach after 18-year-old Loyola University student Sheridan Gorman was shot to death on Thursday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Video captured the man walking from the scene into a building in the 6800 block of West Sheridan Road, according to the arrest report. He wore all black clothing and a mask and had a “distinct gait.”</p><p>The report shows the police department worked closely with U.S. Customs and Border Protection to identify the suspect. CBP, which was integral to the aggressive deportation campaign in Chicago last year, reportedly used facial recognition to identify the man in the video as Medina.</p><p>CBP is a part of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security, which publicly identified Medina as the suspect before charges were approved by Cook County prosecutors. The department also announced that it had issued an arrest detainer for Medina.</p><p>DHS said Medina had been taken into custody by U.S. Border Patrol in May 2023, released and then arrested again two months later for shoplifting. He had an active warrant in the theft case, court records show.</p><p>The Trump administration has used the deaths of other young women to make a case that undocumented immigration puts Americans in danger. However, the efforts to frame crime as the driving force behind its deportation mission have at times been undercut by data and research.</p><p>The killing of Laken Riley, a nursing student in Georgia who was fatally attacked by an immigrant, spurred legislation that mandates detention and strengthens enforcement against undocumented immigrants charged with certain crimes.</p><p>Operation Midway Blitz, the pummeling deportation campaign that tore through the Chicago area last fall, was dedicated to Katie Abraham, an Illinois native killed by a drunken driver who allegedly lacked legal status.</p><p>“Sheridan Gorman's life was allegedly taken from her at the hands of a criminal alien shielded by the anti-American sanctuary policies of the city of Chicago and the state of Illinois,” U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement said on social media Monday.</p><p>Numerous studies have found that immigrants without legal status are less likely to commit serious crimes than U.S.-born citizens.</p><p>A 2024 National Institute of Justice-funded study examining data from the Texas Department of Public Safety found that undocumented immigrants are arrested at less than half the rate of native-born U.S. citizens for violent and drug crimes and a quarter the rate of native-born citizens for property crimes.<a class="Link" href="https://docs.house.gov/meetings/JU/JU01/20250122/117827/HHRG-119-JU01-20250122-SD004.pdf" target="_blank" > </a></p><p>And according to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.migrationpolicy.org/content/immigrants-and-crime" target="_blank" >Migration and Policy Institute</a>, studies have shown that immigrants do not raise crime rates in the local communities where they settle. The institute cites a study by the <a class="Link" href="https://www.umass.edu/preferen/You%20Must%20Read%20This/Lee%20Immigration%20and%20Crime.pdf" target="_blank" >University of Massachusetts</a> that connects lower crime activity, including violent crime, with communities that have inclusive immigration policies.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/19/woman-18-shot-to-death-in-predawn-rogers-park-attack" target="_blank" >Loyola University student, 18, shot to death in predawn attack at Rogers Park beach</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/20/chicago-loyola-sheridan-gorman-rogers-park-crime-shooting-murder" target="_blank" >Friend of slain Loyola student Sheridan Gorman says Rogers Park shooting didn’t feel ‘targeted’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >Venezuelan migrant charged with murder of Loyola student Sheridan Gorman</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Shoes, gun sale led to South Shore shooting that killed 2, wounded 1: report
entries.summary: <p>Two cousins were trying to sell shoes and a gun outside a South Shore apartment building Sunday evening when gunfire erupted, resulting in two men being killed and a third man getting wounded.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/3-people-shot-south-shore" target="_blank" >killings marked the fifth and sixth persons</a> fatally shot over the weekend citywide, which also left nearly a dozen people wounded from gun violence. </p><p>The cousins, men ages 24 and 26, were outside the apartment about 5:20 p.m. in the 6900 block of South South Shore Drive to sell shoes. When the older relative later tried to sell a firearm, someone began shooting, striking him in the chest, according to a Chicago police report.</p><p>He was found on the apartment driveway, while another man, 27, was found about a mile away in the 6800 block of South Constance Avenue with a gunshot wound to the neck, police said. Both were pronounced dead at University of Chicago Medical Center. Their names haven’t been released.</p><p>A rifle and two semiautomatic handguns were found next to the 26-year-old man, the police report said.</p><p>A third man, 32, was shot in the arm and taken to the same hospital in fair condition, police said.</p><p>The 24-year-old man who was selling shoes left the scene after he was held at gunpoint in his car, according to the report.</p><p>A vehicle found in the 6800 block of South Constance Avenue was towed and held for the ongoing investigation, the report said.</p><p>No arrests have been announced.</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: 2026 City/Suburban Hoops Report All-State team
entries.summary: <p><i>As editor/publisher of the City/Suburban Hoops Report, a longtime recruiting service for college basketball programs across the country and a high school basketball publication that began in 1996, there has been an annual 10-player all-state first-team and a 10-player all-state second team for the past 30 years. This is an all-state team that consists of all players and in all classes across the state.</i></p><p><b>First Team </b></p><p><b>Marquis Clark, 6-1, Sr., Young</b></p><p>The Tennessee recruit and top five prospect in the senior class put the Dolphins on his shoulders and carried them to 22 wins on the year. Dynamic in the open floor and beating defenders off the dribble, Clark put up significant numbers and big-time individual performances, averaging over 30 points a game.</p><p>“Marquis is not just a phenomenal player — as evidenced by the way he’s played the game and his commitment to Tennessee — but he’s a refreshing student-athlete that finished where he started,” Young coach Tyrone Slaughter said.</p><p><b>Jaxson Davis, 6-1, Jr., Warren</b></p><p>The state’s top player and prospect, Davis is both the Sun-Times and City/Suburban Hoops Report Player of the Year. The Blue Devils won 30 games as Davis put up 25 points and five assists a game. Already a three-time all-state choice, the polished point guard has surpassed 2,000 career points with a season to play. He will be a coveted recruiting target over the next six months.</p><p>“Jaxson’s shooting percentages increased from all spots on the court from last year. I think that’s a credit to him working on his shooting. He’s gotten better every year, stronger every year, and his efficiency increased a lot from last year. I think he’s also pretty self-aware and knows there’s always areas to get better," Warren coach Zack Ryan said,</p><p><b>Cole Kelly, 6-6, So., Neuqua Valley</b></p><p>The multi-dimensional Kelly was a monster all season. There wasn’t much Kelly didn’t do for a team that finished 32-2 and reached the sectional semifinals. He scored, distributed and rebounded his way to 20 points, eight rebounds and four assists a game. Kelly, who knocked down 70 three-pointers, is a high-major recruit who has cemented himself as a top 100 player in the country.</p><p>“Cole is the most complete player in Illinois. He does everything well. Cole handles his success with grace and humility. He is on a legendary trajectory. I hope the public gets to see what I see every day in practice. I have never seen anything like Cole. Never," Neuqua Valley coach Todd Sutton said.</p><p><b>Brady Pettigrew, 6-3, So., Bolingbrook</b></p><p>With his role and opportunities expanding this season, Pettigrew responded in a big way and led the Raiders to a sectional title game appearance and 25 wins. He put up impressive numbers, averaging 21 points, five rebounds and three assists a game. Pettigrew, a top 50 prospect in the country, also looked the part of the top-ranked prospect in the Class of 2028 in Bolingbrook’s most important games.</p><p>“His game is becoming more mature and expanding by the day. He is the definition of a gym rat. His skill set, coupled with his athleticism, make him one of the top players in the country. Big things are ahead for Brady," Bolingbrook coach Rob Brost said.</p><p><b>Rashaun Porter, 6-7, Sr., DePaul Prep</b></p><p>Porter’s career is rare, even in the long, tradition-rich history of basketball in this state. His four years were all about winning — and at an extremely high level. A generational defensive talent at the high school level, Porter also averaged over 16 points and nearly seven rebounds a game as a senior. Headed to Toledo next season.</p><p>“Come back better at two things. That’s what we ask of our players in the offseason. No one I’ve coached has done a better job of that than Rashaun. From conditioning, finishing, guarding perimeter players and leadership. He’s an all-time great at DePaul," DePaul Prep coach Tom Kleinschmidt said.</p><p><b>Amarion Smith-Holley, 5-11, So., Peoria Richwoods</b></p><p>The eye-opening point guard dazzled all season, to the tune of 21.2 points, 3.7 rebounds, 4.1 assists and 2.3 steals a game. But he put an exclamation point on his sophomore campaign with an incredible postseason run. The Knights won a sectional championship, thanks to the clutch heroics and production of Smith-Holley. He’s one of the top three prospects in the sophomore class.</p><p>“He plays beyond his years. He has a gift of pace, tenacity, maturity, high IQ and a sheer will to win. He is a competitor every day and a kid who strives to get better every day. He is a special talent and only getting better. He was the driving force of our success," Peoria Richwoods coach William Smith said.</p><p><b>Jamison White, 6-8, Sr., East St. Louis</b></p><p>The impact White made in one season is quite impressive. The St. Louis transplant took over the top spot in the Class of 2026 rankings while leading the Flyers to a Class 3A state championship. Even at his size, the Penn State recruit showcased strength, athleticism and skill that led to him averaging 20 points and 12.6 rebounds a game.</p><p>“Jamison is very versatile. He played all five positions for us. And he bought into rebounding and defending at a high level as well. He’s coachable and a great teammate. The most underrated part of his game is his work ethic. He’s in the gym working extra every day. Proud of him for buying in," East St. Louis coach Mark Chambers said.</p><p><b>Lincoln Williams, 6-6, Sr., Kankakee</b></p><p>He leaves Kankakee as arguably the best player in school history, finishing his record-breaking career as the program’s all-time leading scorer with 1,853 points. He also broke the single-season record for points with 704 and single-game record with 44. As a senior, Williams fueled a team that won 29 games and reached the sectional championship. Williams, who put up over 26 points a game, remains the best uncommitted prospect in the senior class.</p><p>“Lincoln’s development and production is a direct result of him being dedicated to the program, the school and the town. He wanted to show people that you have to leave to develop and be challenged. He’ll go down as the most celebrated Kankakee player to date and one of the best players in Illinois over the past two years," Kankakee coach Chris Pickett said.</p><p><b>Rykan Woo, 6-1, Sr., DePaul Prep</b></p><p>All he’s done in his two seasons at DePaul is win 66 games and be a part of a 2025 Class 3A state championship and third-place finisher in 2026. Woo improved and made big strides every year in high school, culminating with a senior season in which he averaged 16.7 points and over four assists a game. The highly-efficient guard, who shot 47 percent from the three-point line, is headed to the Ivy League next year to play at Brown.</p><p>“Rykan is very in-depth and extremely detailed about his game. From his conditioning to his practice habits, watching film, dissecting the opponent and then implementing that to the opponent is next level. It’s rare to see in high school," DePaul Prep coach Tom Kleinschmidt said.</p><p><b>Jayden Wright, 5-10, Sr., Benet</b></p><p>A headline player for a program that over the past two years won a state title, finished second in the state and won 69 games. Known for his shooting range and marksmanship, Wright evolved into a true lead guard. The East Suburban Catholic Conference Player of the Year led the Redwings in scoring (13.5 ppg) and assists (5.5 apg) while making 54 three-pointers. Wright is headed to Eastern Illinois.</p><p>“Jayden had a major impact on both ends of the floor. Whether scoring, handling pressure, creating for his teammates, or defending other top perimeter players, his contributions were an important part of our success," Benet coach Gene Heidkamp said.</p><p><b>Second-Team</b></p><p>Charles Barnes, 6-6, Sr., Marist</p><p>Chris Bolte, 6-6, Sr., St. Ignatius</p><p>Amir Danforth, 6-1, Sr., Rockford Boylan</p><p>Darrius Hawkins Jr., 6-0, So., Homewood-Flossmoor</p><p>Quentin Kitt, 6-6, Jr., East Peoria</p><p>Mason Lockett, 6-6, Sr., Oswego East</p><p>Michael Nee, 6-2, Sr., Glenbard East</p><p>Justin Oliver, 6-0, Sr., Curie</p><p>Jake Pollack, 6-8, Sr., Deerfield</p><p>Colin Stack, 7-1, Sr., Benet</p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Administración de Trump despliega agentes de ICE en aeropuertos y trabajadores del TSA se oponen
entries.summary: <p>Después de que el presidente Donald Trump amenazara con enviar agentes federales de inmigración a los aeropuertos en medio de una disputa por el financiamiento del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) en el Congreso, los miembros del sindicato en el Medio Oeste están expresando su preocupación por la falta de capacitación de los agentes y su propio escaso salario.</p><p>Un día después de afirmar que utilizará oficiales de inmigración para la <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/government-shutdown-tsa-workers-920577c153ffd1877f59dfb8f8efed39" target="_blank" >seguridad aeroportuaria</a> a partir del lunes, a menos que los demócratas aceptaran un proyecto de ley para financiar el <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/us-department-of-homeland-security" target="_blank" >Departamento de Seguridad Nacional</a>, Trump dejó claro el domingo que seguiría adelante con el plan para ayudar a la Administración de Seguridad del Transporte (TSA) .</p><p>Cientos de miles de trabajadores de seguridad nacional, incluyendo los de la TSA, el Servicio Secreto y la Guardia Costera, han <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/tsa-lines-airport-shutdown-food-banks-a1e52ca10557f4f70c1360e878567b4b" target="_blank" >trabajado sin cobrar</a> desde que el Congreso no renovó el financiamiento del DHS el mes pasado. Los demócratas están exigiendo cambios importantes en la conducta de los agentes federales de inmigración.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a30000" name="module-a30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El “zar de la frontera” de la Casa Blanca, Tom Homan, comentó en entrevistas de programas de noticias del domingo que el papel aumentado del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en los aeropuertos estaba sujeto a discusiones con el liderazgo de la TSA y ICE “para averiguar dónde podemos encajar”.</p><p>Los oficiales de inmigración, como ejemplo, podrían cubrir las salidas que actualmente están monitoreadas por agentes de la TSA, liberándolos para trabajar en las filas de inspección, o hacer que los agentes del ICE verifiquen la identificación antes de que las personas entren a las áreas de revisión.</p><p>Homan prometió “un plan para el final del día de hoy, donde vamos a enviar, con qué aeropuertos comenzaremos y a dónde los enviaremos”.</p><p>Funcionarios de DHS e ICE emitieron declaraciones culpando a los demócratas por el "cierre sin sentido y temerario" y afirmaron que la administración de Trump estaba tratando de acortar los tiempos de espera en seguridad en los aeropuertos. Ninguna de las agencias respondió a preguntas sobre el momento del despliegue o el papel que asumirían los agentes.</p><p>La Ciudad de Chicago y su Departamento de Aviación no respondieron de inmediato a una solicitud de comentarios.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b1c806" name="image-b1c806"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fabed1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7772x4362+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F38%2F7814d73b447fb413153d798abf3d%2Ficeairports-260323-009.jpg alt="Los viajeros esperan en las filas de seguridad en el Aeropuerto Internacional O'Hare de Chicago, en el lado noroeste, el domingo 22 de marzo de 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/511cde1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7772x4362+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F38%2F7814d73b447fb413153d798abf3d%2Ficeairports-260323-009.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb383a8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7772x4362+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F38%2F7814d73b447fb413153d798abf3d%2Ficeairports-260323-009.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/511cde1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7772x4362+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F38%2F7814d73b447fb413153d798abf3d%2Ficeairports-260323-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los viajeros esperan en las filas de seguridad en el Aeropuerto Internacional O’Hare de Chicago, en el lado noroeste, el domingo 22 de marzo de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Everett Kelley, el presidente nacional de la Federación Estadounidense de Empleados Gubernamentales (AFGE), que representa a los agentes de la TSA y otros trabajadores federales en Illinois y Wisconsin, dijo que el despliegue de los agentes plantea preocupaciones de seguridad para los pasajeros.</p><p>“Nuestros miembros de la TSA se presentan todos los días, sin un sueldo, porque creen en la misión de mantener a la población voladora segura”, dijo Kelley en un comunicado. “Merecen que se les pague, no ser reemplazados por agentes armados y sin capacitación que han demostrado cuán peligrosos pueden ser”.</p><p>Darrell English —presidente del Local 777 de la AFGE, que representa a los trabajadores de la TSA en Chicago— dijo que el peligro también se extiende a la falta de capacitación de los agentes en temas de seguridad aérea. Agregar agentes del ICE podría ralentizar el proceso de revisión para los viajeros, ya que carecen de los siete meses de capacitación que los agentes de la TSA reciben antes de poder realizar revisiones de manera independiente, según English.</p><p>“Siempre es preocupante cuando escuchas un lenguaje así en relación con el cambio de la seguridad que se está implementando”, dijo English. “Deja un vacío... la TSA comprende la amenaza para la aviación y para la población voladora, y también toma años de comprensión para ser eficiente y seguro”.</p><p>Al responder a críticas similares, Homan dijo que “ciertamente, un oficial de la ley de ICE altamente capacitado puede cubrir una salida... cosas así alivian a ese oficial de la TSA para que vaya a las verificaciones y reduzca esas filas”.</p><p>“Los agentes de ICE ya están asignados en muchos aeropuertos del país”, afirmó Homan. “[Pero] no veo a un agente de ICE mirando una máquina de rayos X, porque no estamos capacitados para eso”.</p><p>La gran mayoría de los empleados de la TSA son considerados esenciales y han continuado trabajando sin salario durante la interrupción del financiamiento. Las tasas de ausentismo han comenzado a aumentar en algunos aeropuertos, y DHS dijo que al menos 376 han renunciado desde que comenzó el cierre parcial el 14 de febrero.</p><p>El Senado rechazó el sábado una moción de los demócratas para abordar la legislación que financie la TSA. Los republicanos argumentan que necesitan financiar todas las partes de DHS.</p><p>Si la disputa de financiamiento no se resuelve para el viernes, será el segundo sueldo perdido para los trabajadores federales afectados. English afirmó que estaba al tanto de varios oficiales dentro del Local 777 que se fueron durante el prolongado cierre del año pasado así como durante el más reciente, aunque no tenía un conteo específico.</p><p>“Si no pueden cuidar de su familia debido a los problemas financieros con la TSA, buscarán diferentes alternativas”, dijo English, instando a otros a comunicarse con sus representantes federales al respecto. “Estos son años de capacitación que se pierden cuando estos veteranos se van”.</p><p><i>Con información de AP </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por</i> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: 2026 City/Suburban Hoops Report Player of the Year: Warren's Jaxson Davis
entries.summary: <p><i>As Editor/Publisher of the City/Suburban Hoops Report, a longtime recruiting service for college basketball programs across the country and a high school basketball publication that began in 1996, I have awarded a Player of the Year in Illinois for the past 30 years. Here is the 31st recipient of the award.</i></p><p>With three high school basketball seasons in the books, Jaxson Davis has become a no-doubter and in a multitude of ways.</p><p>The Warren star is no doubt the best player in the state. He’s no-doubt a high-major college prospect. And with 2,000-plus points already scored, a state runner-up finish as a sophomore and 88 wins in three years, he’s no doubt put himself in the discussion of being an all-time great in this state.</p><p>Even with the season ending prematurely in the sectional — earlier than the previous two years — the junior guard put together yet another fantastic season. He led the Blue Devils with 25 points, six rebounds and five assists a game while helping them win 30 games.</p><p>With a chance to become the first player ever to score 3,000 points in Lake County history, Davis could also make a significant climb up the state scoring records during his senior year.</p><p>There are only six players in state history to finish their careers with 3,000-plus points. The most recent one to do so was Jon Scheyer at Glenbrook North, who is the fifth all-time leading scorer in state history with 3,034 points.</p><p>Davis took some time to discuss a whole lot of different topics. The conversation included the tough, unfortunate ending to his season, the continued drive to win a state championship, staying humble and hopefully adding some “Blue Blood” attention to his recruitment.</p><p>Here is Davis, the City/Suburban Hoops Report’s Player of the Year and Chicago Sun-Times Player of the Year, in his words.</p><p><b>"The ending of last year just kept playing in my head while sitting there in State Farm Center.</b> There was definitely some bitterness. I was down there [IHSA State Finals] on Saturday to get the first-team all-state award and recognition. I saw the 3A game, but I got out of there before the 4A game."</p><p><b>"The ending this year was tough. It was really tough. I got hurt in practice, so it really wasn’t in my hands.</b> My freshman year and last year it really, really hurt losing those games because the game was right there in my hands. I was 100 percent fully able to be out there. But it hit different this year, because I wasn’t able to be myself, play like myself after getting hurt in practice. I was hit in the head in practice [the day before the game]. Tried to battle it out. I wanted to play it out with Braylon. I’ve been playing with him since third or fourth grade. That really hit hard."</p><p><b>"It was bad. People thought it was my vision.</b> There was that sectional feel and all loud and my head was pounding. I couldn’t keep my focus, nothing felt right. My head was pulsating. I had a headache for three or four days."</p><p><b>"People talk about how fast it will go and don’t take it for granted, but I feel like I see it right before my eyes.</b> Like it’s crazy that I’m going to be a senior next year and it’s my last ride. It really doesn’t mean anything unless you win at the end, so that’s the goal. It’s extremely important."</p><p><b>"I’ve accomplished a lot as a player, not everything, but a lot. The only thing left off is a state championship</b>. It’s really the only thing that matters to me now, especially with it being my senior year next year. It motivates me a lot."</p><p><b>"The enjoyment I get playing in high school and in AAU is displaying what I’ve been working on as a player.</b> There are a lot of days and a lot of nights in the gym. It gets tough and is a lot of work. So every time I step on the court and eyes are on me, I feel like the enjoyment is to go play free and to show all that I’ve been working on and display that on the floor."</p><p><b>"The goal in the spring and summer is to have Blue Blood programs take a look and start contacting me, play well in EYBL, and hopefully get an invite to the USA trials in June.</b> I feel like I hit the weight room really hard last year, but I think that will be a focus for me in the offseason, hitting it even harder. I know at the next level you need to be bigger and stronger, obviously."</p><p><b>"The Big Ten schools are really pushing me hard. Purdue, Michigan State, Michigan, Illinois, Iowa and Marquette in the Big East.</b> I would say those are the five or six schools that have been recruiting me the hardest."</p><p><b>"I want to be part of a winning program and be at a place that can really develop players.</b> I really watch what the point guards are doing at these schools and how they utilize them, how they develop their point guards. I want to win, but I want to also fulfill my dreams of playing at the next level in the NBA, so development will be important."</p><p><b>"Being mentioned with past great high school players, it’s an extreme honor.</b> It’s a huge honor to be mentioned with those names, those types of guys at an early age. With how much those players have accomplished, and that they are playing at the highest level, I don’t take it for granted in being mentioned with them. I’m not there yet, but it feels good that my name has been included with those at this point."</p><p><b>"I just know I need to stay humble and stay in the gym.</b> I’ve been in gyms where I haven’t been the best guy or the best player, so there are always things to push for. There is a lot ahead of me. There are places I want to go that I haven’t yet."</p><p><b>"Staying humble. That came from my parents at an early age.</b> When I was younger, I was pretty good at basketball, but my parents and grandparents always told me to stay humble, even at a really young age. I feel like that was naturally ingrained in me. I watch players at the college level that are humble, and I think people really like and appreciate that. Stay grounded. Stay humble."</p><p><b>Past City/Suburban Hoops Report Player of Year Winners</b></p><p>2026: Jaxson Davis, Warren</p><p>2025: Davion Thompson, Bolingbrook</p><p>2024: Morez Johnson, Thornton</p><p>2023: Brock Harding, Moline</p><p>2022: Braden Huff, Glenbard West</p><p>2021: Max Christie, Rolling Meadows</p><p>2020: DJ Steward, Young</p><p>2019: EJ Liddell, Belleville West</p><p>2018: Talen Horton-Tucker, Simeon</p><p>2017: Mark Smith, Edwardsville</p><p>2016: Charlie Moore, Morgan Park</p><p>2015: Jalen Brunson, Stevenson</p><p>2014: Jahlil Okafor, Young</p><p>2013: Jahlil Okafor, Young</p><p>2012: Jabari Parker, Simeon</p><p>2011: Wayne Blackshear, Morgan Park</p><p>2010: Jereme Richmond, Waukegan</p><p>2009: Drew Crawford, Naperville Central</p><p>2008: Kevin Dillard, Homewood-Flossmoor</p><p>2007: Derrick Rose, Simeon</p><p>2006: Jon Scheyer, Glenbrook North</p><p>2005: Jon Scheyer, Glenbrook North</p><p>2004: Shaun Livingston, Peoria Central</p><p>2003: Shannon Brown, Proviso East</p><p>2002: Dee Brown, Proviso East</p><p>2001: Pierre Pierce, Westmont</p><p>2000: Dwyane Wade, Richards</p><p>1999: Leon Smith, King</p><p>1998: Quentin Richardson, Young</p><p>1997: Brian Wardle, Hinsdale Central</p><p>1996: Ronnie Fields, Farragut</p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Migrante venezolano acusado de asesinato de la estudiante de la Universidad Loyola
entries.summary: <p>Un migrante venezolano ha sido acusado del asesinato de Sheridan Gorman, estudiante de la Universidad Loyola de Chicago, anunciaron las autoridades policiales el domingo, lo que provocó la indignación de funcionarios de la administración de Trump, quienes calificaron su asesinato como el resultado de las políticas de santuario en Illinois.</p><p>José Medina, de 25 años, enfrenta cargos de asesinato en primer grado, intento de asesinato y descarga agravada de un arma de fuego por el ataque del 19 de marzo cerca de un muelle en la playa Tobey Prinz, según la Policía de Chicago.</p><p>Gorman, de 18 años, y sus amigos dieron un paseo el jueves por la mañana justo al norte del campus de Rogers Park, con la esperanza de tomar algunas fotos con vista al horizonte y potencialmente a las Auroras Boreales, dijo su familia en un comunicado.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6c0000" name="module-6c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Cuando llegaron, un hombre enmascarado “emergió de las sombras” y disparó un solo tiro mientras ellos huían, según un informe de la Policía de Chicago. Uno de los amigos de Gorman <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/20/chicago-loyola-sheridan-gorman-rogers-park-crime-shooting-murder" target="_blank" >que fue testigo del tiroteo</a> le contó al Chicago Sun-Times que “no parecía que estuviera dirigido intencionalmente hacia ninguno de nosotros”.</p><p>Los amigos corrieron en diferentes direcciones cuando se disparó, pero volvieron al ver a Gorman en el suelo. Ella había recibido un tiro en el torso y fue <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/19/woman-18-shot-to-death-in-predawn-rogers-park-attack" target="_blank" >declarada muerta en la escena</a>, informaron las autoridades.</p><p>Las autoridades federales de inmigración informaron que el Servicio de Inmigración y Aduanas (ICE) emitió un aviso de detención que nombraba a Medina horas antes de que la policía anunciara los cargos.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-76c6e6" name="image-76c6e6"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
